title: Colin Farrell Says He 'Hasn't Got Much to Do' in 'The Batman Part II'

blocks.content: Caroline Preece has more than 15 years of experience writing entertainment content. Her work for outlets including Den of Geek, Backstage, Tom’s Guide, CultBox, and Starburst prioritizes sharp analysis and reader-first reporting with a particular interest in the ways storytelling, audience behavior, and digital culture drive modern entertainment trends.

Matt Reeves's Gotham of The Batman has spent the past two years consolidating around a single breakout performance, and that kind of villain turn usually signals a much larger role waiting down the line. The next chapter is confirmed to land in October 2027, and the actor behind the popular performance has just given an honest account of his involvement – note, it's not what the build-up may have led fans to expect.

Robert Pattinson returns under the cowl, joined by a stack of new faces. Sebastian Stan plays Harvey Dent, the district attorney destined to become Two-Face, with Scarlett Johansson as his wife, Gilda, and Charles Dance as his father. Brian Tyree Henry and Sebastian Koch have also boarded, while Andy Serkis returns as Alfred and Jeffrey Wright as Jim Gordon. The film's primary villain remains under wraps, in keeping with Reeves's hope to build an antagonist that's "never really been done in a movie before."

And, yes, Colin Farrell, who reshaped Oswald "Oz" Cobb into a Golden Globe, Critics' Choice, and SAG-winning standout for The Penguin, is heading back to Gotham for The Batman Part II. Though he's not, by his own account, heading back for very long.

Speaking to Collider, Farrell said he will be flying to London in four or five weeks' time to shoot his scenes, but that there aren't a great many of them. "I haven't got much to do on it, but I'll go for four or five weeks," he said, before adding that he's "so excited to see it as a fan." For the man whose spin-off drew 24 Emmy nominations and carried him through awards season, it sounds like a strikingly low-key return.

What he may lack in screen time, he makes up for in enthusiasm for the script. Farrell called Reeves's screenplay "extraordinary," having previously described it on the Happy Sad Confused podcast as "a contemporary genre masterwork" that is "dense" and "really, really intelligent." Reeves co-wrote the sequel with Mattson Tomlin, and Farrell singled out the director's care for the material, praising how deeply he "cares about the stories he tells."

Farrell has been flagging a limited role for a while. Speaking after the series wrapped, he revealed he'd signed on for three Batman films but was told he had only five or six scenes in the sequel, so a short London stint lines up with what he's said all along. It also fits with what Reeves has described, with the follow-up pivoting toward Bruce Wayne rather than circling the rogues' gallery.

The first film grossed $772 million worldwide, so a sequel was never in doubt, even with five years between installments. Farrell's reduced role does reframe Oz, though, from the franchise's breakout engine to a supporting piece in someone else's story. Whether that's a one-film breather or a quarantine of the character for the small screen, the actor sounds happy enough to take in the franchise as a fan.
